coredata.dc:title: Enhanced photocatalytic performance of Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid sensitized by black phosphorus nanosheets in visible and near-infrared light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xin
Xiang, Yuren
Zhou, Benqing
Zhang, Youming
Wu, Jiatao
Hu, Rui
Liu, Liwei
Song, Jun
Qu, Junle
coredata.dc:description: The efficient utilization of solar energy for environmental cleaning has attracted great attention, where the key is to efficiently harvest the visible and near-infrared (NIR) light which occupies approximately 95% of the solar light energy. Recently, black phosphorus (BP), as a new staring 2D material, has been extensively studied as photocatalytic materials due to its broad light absorption and tunable bandgap. Herein, we report a novel ternary nanocomposite, BP-Ag/TiO2, prepared through controlled deposition of Ag clusters on the surface of TiO2 nanocrystals and then incorporated to BP nanosheets. The BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite has shown excellent photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible and NIR light irradiation. About 100% and even 25% of MB was degraded in 85 min under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite was mainly ascribed to the sensitization of BP nanosheets by fully harvest of solar light and high electron-hole separation efficiency. We believe that the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite will be an effective photofunctional material in full-spectrum solar energy conversion and opens up a new door for the development of solar light driven photocatalysts for the remediation of environmental pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Black phosphorus nanosheets
BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite
Photocatalysis
Ag clusters
Methylene blue

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of the as-synthesized TiO2 nanocrystals. The inset in (a) shows the HRTEM image of a single TiO2 nanocrystal (scale bar = 2 nm). (b) The plots of Amax against the reaction time for Ag/TiO2 and the Ag nanocrystals, respectively. The inset shows the reduction process of the Ag precursor with and without the TiO2 nanocrystals. (c and d) Ultra-HRSEM images of the as-obtained Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite with low (c) and high (d) magnification. The inset in (d) shows the corresponding size-distribution of the as-obtained Ag clusters on the surface of the TiO2 nanocrystals.

(a) The TEM image of the as-obtained BP nanosheets after ultra-sonication for 2 h and centrifugation at 2000 rpm/min for 20 min. (b) The TEM image of the as-obtained BP nanosheets after centrifugation at 7000 rpm/min for 20 min. (c) The AFM image of the as-obtained BP nanosheets. (d) The height profiles along the red lines shown in (c). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a and b) The TEM image of the as-obtained BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposites with low (a) and high (b) magnification. (c) HRTEM image of the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid. (d) EDX spectra of the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid obtained in a.

(a) The STEM image of the as-obtained BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposites. (b) P, O, Ti and Ag mapping image. (c) P element mapping image. (d) O element mapping image. (e) Ti element mapping image. (f) Ag element mapping image.
The UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and the Raman spectra (b) of the as-obtained BP nanosheets, Ag/TiO2 nanohybrids and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposites.

(a) The full scan XPS spectrum of the ternary BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite. (b) The high-resolution Ag 3d spectrum of the Ag/TiO2 nanohybrids. (c and d) The high-resolution P2p XPS spectra of the bulk BP crystals (c) and BP nanosheets (d).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of pure MB solution as a function of irradiation time (under >420 nm irradiation). (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of MB solution as a function of irradiation time (under >420 nm irradiation) in the presence of the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite used as a photocatalyst.

Normalized absorption maximum A t/A 0 of MB solution in the presence of the BP nanosheets, Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite used as photocatalysts, as compared with no catalyst (under >420 nm irradiation).

(a) The UV–vis absorption spectra of MB solution as a function of irradiation time (under >780 nm irradiation) in the presence of the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite used as a photocatalyst. (b) Normalized absorption maximum A t/A 0 of MB solution in the presence of the BP nanosheets, Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite used as photocatalysts, as compared with no catalyst (under >780 nm irradiation).

(a and b) The plots of A t/A 0 against the irradiation time using the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite as the photocatalyst in three successive photodegradation reaction cycles under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively. (c and d) The corresponding plots of ln[A t/A 0] against the irradiation time using the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite as the photocatalyst in three successive photodegradation reaction cycles under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively.

Rate constants (k) for photocatalytic degradation process of MB with BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite as the photocatalyst in three successive reaction cycles under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively.

(a) Photocurrent responses of Ag/TiO2, BP-Ag/TiO2 under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively. (b) DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra with BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite in water (for DMPO-
O2 −) under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively.
Schematic diagram illustrating the transfer of the photogenerated charges of the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite under solar light irradiation.

A schematic illustration of the synthesis of the ternary BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite. (a) The synthesis of Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid, (b) The synthesis of the BP nanosheets and subsequently deposited with Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid.
The apparent rate constant K · 10−3 (min−1) of Ag/TiO2, GO-Ag/TiO2 and BP-Ag/TiO2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The K value of Ag/TiO2 was calculated in 35 min, as it lost photocatalytic activity after 35 min.

head.ce:title: Enhanced photocatalytic performance of Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid sensitized by black phosphorus nanosheets in visible and near-infrared light

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biophotonics Research Laboratory, Center of Interdisciplinary Biomedical Education and Research, College of Mathematics and Science, University of Central Oklahoma, OK 73034, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Interdisciplinary Biomedical Education and Research

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hung Hom, Hong Kong 999077, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Biology and Chemical Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The efficient utilization of solar energy for environmental cleaning has attracted great attention, where the key is to efficiently harvest the visible and near-infrared (NIR) light which occupies approximately 95% of the solar light energy. Recently, black phosphorus (BP), as a new staring 2D material, has been extensively studied as photocatalytic materials due to its broad light absorption and tunable bandgap. Herein, we report a novel ternary nanocomposite, BP-Ag/TiO2, prepared through controlled deposition of Ag clusters on the surface of TiO2 nanocrystals and then incorporated to BP nanosheets. The BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite has shown excellent photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible and NIR light irradiation. About 100% and even 25% of MB was degraded in 85 min under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic activity of BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite was mainly ascribed to the sensitization of BP nanosheets by fully harvest of solar light and high electron-hole separation efficiency. We believe that the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite will be an effective photofunctional material in full-spectrum solar energy conversion and opens up a new door for the development of solar light driven photocatalysts for the remediation of environmental pollution.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy and environmental issues are of great significance in social and economic developments, where many researchers devoted to the utilization of renewable energy for environmental remediation [1–4]. Solar energy, as a green and inexhaustible resource, has been considered as one of the most promising renewable sources of energy. For example, utilizing solar light in an efficient approach to degrade harmful and toxic dyes through photocatalysis has been of great concern and extensively investigated [5–8]. However, most of the previously reported photocatalysts, such as TiO2, have a wide band gap (>3.2 eV), which means that only UV light could be used to activate their photocatalytic activities [4,9]. It is also known that in the solar spectrum, UV light contributes to <5% of the energy, while the most part lies in the visible range (<45%) and the near-infrared (NIR) range (about 50%). As a result, to increase the efficiency of solar-energy utilization, efforts to extend the light response of the photocatalysts from UV to visible and NIR range have been made [10]. Over the past few decades, a series of visible light and NIR light driven photocatalysts such as RGO/Ag2S/TiO2 nanohybrids, WS2, up-conversion nanoparticles, Ag/AgCl@ZIF8 heterostructures and Ag/AgCl@MIL-101 nanohybrids have been developed [8,11–14]. Unfortunately, most of them either display low photo-energy harvesting efficiency or are difficult to synthesize. It still remains a challenge to design photocatalysts capable of harvesting sunlight covering the visible and the NIR regions.

Ultra-thin black phosphorus (BP), as a rapidly rising star in the materials field, has been intensively investigated since early 2014. It has a 2D layered structure with tunable band gap in the range of 0.3–2.0 eV, which enables its broad light absorption from the visible to NIR region [15–17]. Recently, Sa et al. investigated the potential application of BP in photocatalytic hydrogen production from water splitting based on density functional theory calculations [18]. Liu et al. have reported a novel Ag/BP plasmonic nanohybrid with experimental studies as well as numerical simulations, where the as-prepared Ag/BP nanohybrid showed enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of RhB [19]. The Majima group developed a novel BP-Au/LTO nanostructure, which was used as an efficient and stable visible and NIR light-driven photocatalyst for H2 production [20].

Herein, a ternary BP-sensitized Ag/TiO2 (BP-Ag/TiO2) nanostructure was synthesized for the first time and used as an efficient and stable visible and NIR light-driven photocatalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of MB. The as-obtained BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite was characterized using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, atomic force microscopy (AFM), Raman microscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultra-high resolution scanning electron microscopy (Ultra-HRSEM), high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and so on. The results demonstrate that the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite can be effectively sensitized by BP nanosheets to obtain an enhanced photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of MB under visible and NIR light irradiation with a 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm and 780 nm long-pass cut-off filter. This enhanced photocatalytic activity of degradation of MB is ascribed to the sensitization of BP nanosheets, which serve as a novel semiconductor to fully harvest visible and NIR light.

The synthesis of TiO2 nanocrystals has been previously reported using a two-phase solvothermal method. In this synthesis, about 3 mL of tert-butylamine was added to 300 mL of water and the mixing solution was transferred to a 1 L Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. Subsequently, 30 mL of oleic acid, 5 mL of n-butyltitanate and 300 mL of toluene were added to the autoclave, respectively. Then, the sealed autoclave was heated to 180 °C and held at this temperature for 720 min, and then allowed to cool to room temperature naturally. The obtained crude solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm/min to remove the solid impurities and the upper supernatant solution containing the TiO2 nanocrystals was precipitated upon the addition of ethanol and further isolated using centrifugation. The purified TiO2 nanocrystals were re-dispersed in toluene for further use.

In this synthesis, firstly, 0.34 g of AgNO3 and 1 g of 1-dodecylamine were added to 20 mL of a mixed solution comprised of toluene and ethanol (volume ratio = 3:1) under vigorous stirring to form a stock solution of silver-dodecylamine complexes (silver precursor). Then, a certain amount of silver precursor solution and 100 mg of TiO2 nanocrystals solution were added to 30 mL of toluene in a 100 mL round bottom flask at 50 °C. Subsequently, a certain amount of acetaldehyde/toluene solution (about 8 mol/L, 0.25 mL) was added to initiate the reduction. During the reduction, 0.25 mL of the sample solution was taken and diluted with 3 mL toluene for characterization using UV–vis spectroscopy. The resultant solution was precipitated upon the addition of ethanol and submitted to a cycle of centrifugation, re-dispersion and precipitation. This procedure was repeated three times to remove the oleic acid absorbed on the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite (Scheme 1 a).

The BP nanosheets were obtained using an N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) solvent exfoliation process. 20 mg of bulk BP was added into 20 mL of NMP. The dispersion was sonicated for 2 h using a tip sonicator (Misonix XL-2000) at a power output of 10 W with cooling in an ice-water bath. The dispersion was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 20 min to remove the non-exfoliated bulk BP, the remaining filtrate was further sonicated in bath for 2 h and centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 20 min to remove BP quantum dots dispersion solution, resulting in the BP nanosheets product.

The BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite was prepared by adding 12 mg of Ag/TiO2 into 10 mL of NMP with sonication to form a homogeneous dispersion. Afterwards, 15 mL of the as-prepared BP NMP dispersion was added into the Ag/TiO2 dispersion with sonication for 2 h. The resulting mixture was stirred overnight. The powders were collected using high-speed centrifugation, thoroughly washed with ethanol and then dried in an oven at 40 °C for 0.5 h to give the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite (Scheme 1b). For comparison, Ag/TiO2 deposited on Graphene oxide (GO) for BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite was also synthesized by replacing BP nanosheets with GO.

UV–vis spectra were recorded in the range from 200 to 800 nm using a Shimadzu UV-1780 spectrometer. AFM measurements were carried out using a BRUKER-MultiMode 8 microscope. Raman Microscopy was performed using a Renishaw INVIA REFLEX HR Evolution confocal Raman spectrometer equipped with a microscope and an automated XYZ–table (excitation wavelengths 457 nm) with a laser spot size of ∼1 μm. TEM images were recorded on a Philips Tecnai G2 Spirit microscope operating at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV, HRTEM image was obtained using a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 Spirit microscope operated at 300 kV accelerating voltage, and the samples were prepared by dropping a diluted dispersion of the sample onto a 200 mesh carbon-coated copper grid. Ultra-HRSEM images were obtained using a HITACHI S5500 microscope operated at 30 kV. XPS spectra were obtained using X-ray photoelectron Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with a Al Kα radiation as the X-ray source (U = 15 kv, I = 12.8 mA), scanning resolution is 0.1 eV. The spectra were calibrated to the C 1s peak at 284.5 eV. Photocurrent measurements were performed on a M304979 electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode cell with the nanostructure materials on FTO as the working electrode, saturated calomel electrode and platinum electrode as the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The electron spin resonce (ESR) signals were examined on a Bruker ER200-SRC-10/12 spectrometer under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB)
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid was evaluated towards the degradation of MB at room temperature under ambient conditions. For the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst, 5 mL of MB (1 × 10−3 M) was placed into 45 mL of the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid supernatant (1 mg/mL). Subsequently, the solution was sonicated for 10 min and magnetically stirred in the dark for 30 min to ensure the complete adsorption-desorption equilibrium of MB on the surface of the photocatalyst. A 300 W xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm and 780 nm long-pass filter was used. The light intensity in the center of the beaker was measured to be about 300 mW/cm2 using a Newport optical power/energy meter (842-PE). During the illumination, the reaction temperature was maintained at room temperature. At certain time intervals, about 0.5 mL of the suspension was collected and centrifuged at 14,800 rpm for 3 min to remove the catalyst. The photoactivity was monitored by measuring the solution absorbance at the maximum wavelength of 660 nm (MB) using a Shimadzu UV-1780 spectrophotometer. For the successive MB degradation reactions, when one round of the reaction was finished, another 5 mL of MB dye was added to the reaction solution for a next reaction cycle. This reaction was repeated three times to study the catalytic stability of the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite.

Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite with tiny Ag clusters planted on the surface of TiO2 nanocrystals was synthesized using a modified silver mirror reaction in toluene, which refers to the reduction of silver–dodecylamine complexes by acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) in the presence of TiO2 nanocrystals (Scheme 1a) [21,22]. In this synthesis, TiO2 nanocrystals were synthesized using a two-phase solvothermal method with sizes in the range of 4–6 nm (Fig. 1 a) and enclosed by (1 0 1) facets, as confirmed from the lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.35 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). During the reduction, we recorded the absorption spectra maximum (Amax) of the reaction solution as a function of the reaction time (t). As shown in Fig. 1b, we can find that the absorbance quickly increased with prolonging the reaction time, indicating the continuous deposition of Ag atoms on the surface of the TiO2 nanocrystals [23]. In sharp contrast, the pure silver mirror reaction in toluene without the addition of TiO2 nanocrystals can be separated into two parts of nuclei formation and crystal growth. The formation of Ag nuclei was an energy-consuming process, so the increase in absorbance occurs at a low rate. After the Ag nucleation, crystal growth occurs with a high rate as catalyzed by the Ag nuclei [24,25]. We used Ultra-HRSEM to discriminate the Ag clusters on the TiO2 nanocrystals from the as-obtained Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite. As illustrated in Fig. 1c and d, we can clearly see that the Ag clusters have sizes in the range of 3–4 nm (3.5 nm in average, inset of Fig. 1d), which were uniformly decorated on the surface of the TiO2 nanocrystals. Moreover, no large size or free-standing Ag nanoparticles were observed, indicating that the emerging Ag atoms are all deposited on the surface of the TiO2 nanocrystals while there is no self-nucleation and subsequent growth of the nuclei into large-sized Ag nanoparticles.

Although TiO2 is widely used as an effective photocatalyst and its photocatalytic activity can be enhanced by the deposition of Ag clusters on its surface, its applications in photocatalytic reactions in the visible and NIR light range is still limited due to its low light response in this range. To improve this situation, BP nanosheets was introduced as a photosensitizer, which was reported as an effective visible and NIR responsive optical material [26,27]. Ultra-thin BP nanosheets were prepared by exfoliating BP layered crystals in NMP (Scheme 1b) [15–17]. After probe sonication for about 2 h, we can obtain a mixture of BP nanosheets and BPQDs as shown in Fig. 2 a, where BPQDs with sizes of about 5 nm were adsorbed on the surface of the BP nanosheets. Subsequent bath sonication for another 2 h removes the free BPQDs after centrifugation to obtain the pure BP nanosheets (Fig. 2b). The morphology, thickness and surface properties of the BP nanosheets were characterized using AFM. Fig. 2c shows a typical AFM image of the BP nanosheets and the corresponding height profiles are displayed in Fig. 2d. We can clearly see that the size of the BP nanosheets is about 50–200 nm, which was consistent with the TEM results shown in Fig. 2b. The thickness of the BP nanosheets predominantly lies at 5–6 nm, which corresponds to approximately 10–12 atomic layers.

To ensure the dispersion of the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite on the BP nanosheets, TEM and HRTEM were also carried out. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, we can clearly see that the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite was highly dispersed on the surface of a single BP nanosheet while no large size free-standing particles can be observed, which was consistent with the results shown in Fig. 1c and d. The HRTEM image in Fig. 3c can further confirm the TiO2 nanocrystals enclosed with (1 0 1) facets (d = 0.35 nm) and tiny Ag clusters dispersed on the surface of the BP nanosheets. EDX analysis in Fig. 3d shows that the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid contained the elements P and Ti without the presence of Ag, which can be ascribed to the formation of small-sized Ag clusters and the Ag content being below the detection limit. Also, we choose a region from a scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) image for element mapping analysis (Fig. 4 a and b). Clearly, P, O, Ti and Ag elements are all detected and uniformly dispersed as shown in Fig. 4c–f, thus, we can confirm that Ag clusters anchored on the surface of TiO2 nanosrystals and then highly dispersed on BP nanosheet surfaces.

The UV–vis absorption spectra of the as-synthesized Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite, BP nanosheets and ternary BP-Ag/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 5 a. For the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite, an absorbance peak centered at about 420 nm was observed, which was ascribed to the characteristic plasmonic resonance peak of the Ag species. In addition, a tail towards the long wavelength region was also observed, which was ascribed to the interparticle coupling effect between the neighbouring Ag clusters anchored on one TiO2 nanocrystal [22]. The tail can further enhance the ability of the nanocomposites to capture the visible-light in the region of about 500–600 nm. Moreover, for the BP nanosheets, we can clearly see that the BP nanosheets display a broad absorption over the entire visible and NIR region covering 300–950 nm. The deposition of the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite on the BP nanosheets can effectively combine the light response of the Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite and BP nanosheets.

The Raman spectra of the bulk BP, BP nanosheets and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite are illustrated in Fig. 5b. The Raman spectrum of bulk BP exhibits three peaks located at 361.3, 437.5 and 465.3 cm−1, which are assigned to one out-of-plane phonon mode Ag 1 as well as two in-plane modes, B2g and Ag 2, respectively. These signals were also observed in the Raman spectra of the BP nanosheets and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite, suggesting the BP nanosheets and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite maintain the same vibrational structure of bulk BP. However, the three Raman peaks are apparently broadened for the BP nanosheets and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite, indicating that the as-obtained BP nanosheets are a mixture of nanosheet structures with variable thicknesses. Moreover, a slight blue-shift can be observed for the BP nanosheets and BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite when compared with the bulk BP, which has also been found in the literature, indicating the successful exfoliation process [28].

We also performed XPS analysis to investigate the surface composition of the as-obtained BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 6 a, both Ag and Ti were detected on the surface of the BP nanosheets, since the double peaks at around 370 eV and 460 eV were assigned to the Ag 3d and Ti 2p energy levels, respectively. Moreover, the binding energy of the Ag clusters on the TiO2 nanocrystals shown in Fig. 6b (Ag 3d peak at 367.7 eV and 373.7 eV) is lower than that of metallic Ag (Ag 3d peak at 368.2 eV and 374.2 eV), indicating that the emerging tiny Ag clusters have been strongly bound to the surface of the TiO2 nanocrystals instead of a simple adsorption of metallic Ag particles on the surface of the TiO2 nanocrystals [21]. The high-resolution P 2p XPS spectra of the bulk BP and BP nanosheets are compared in Fig. 6c and d, showing quite similar features with a sharp peak at 129.7 and a shoulder peak at 130.5 eV, as well as a broad peak in the region of 132.0–136.0 eV. Fitting analysis shows that the P 2p peaks of both the bulk BP and BP nanosheets can be deconvoluted into five peaks at 129.7, 130.5, 131.6, 132.8 and 134.3 eV. The peaks at 129.7 and 130.5 can be assigned to P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 of the PP bonds, which are characteristic of crystalline BP. Also, the peaks at 131.6, 132.8, and 134.3 eV can be ascribed to POP (bridging bonding), OPO (dangling bonding) and P2O5, originating from oxygen defects or surface suboxides on the BP surfaces [29]. Moreover, the peaks ascribed to the oxidized phosphorus in the BP nanosheets are more apparent than those observed in bulk BP, indicating that the BP nanosheets are more active to oxygen than bulk BP and can be oxidized during the solvent exfoliation process [30,31].

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite, the degradation of MB dye was carried out as a model reaction. As a comparison, BP nanosheets and Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite with comparable Ag loading were also synthesized and used in this study [32–34]. Typically, an aqueous solution of MB (50 mL, 1 × 10−4 M) mixed with the photocatalyst (50 mg) was irradiated under visible and NIR light using a xenon lamp equipped with a 420 nm long-pass filter. We firstly carried out a blank experiment in the absence of a photocatalyst as illustrated in Fig. 7 a, which shows almost no photocatalytic activity. Fig. 7b shows the spectra of the MB solution as a function of time over the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite catalyst. The absorption peak at about 660 nm, which was ascribed to the characteristic peak of MB, decreased upon increasing the irradiation time, which was an indication of the decomposition of MB. After 85 min, almost no significant absorbance peak was observed, which indicated the complete degradation of MB via the photocatalytic reaction of the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite. To quantify the photocatalytic reaction for different nanocomposite combinations, the normalized absorption maximum of MB (At/A0) was plotted against the reaction time and shown in Fig. 8 , where At and A0 correspond to the peak absorbance of MB at time t and 0, respectively. We can clearly see that the photocatalytic degradation of MB hardly occurred under the irradiation of the xenon lamp in the absence of a photocatalyst. The introduction of pure BP nanosheets can lead to the absorbance of the MB solution to decrease by 20% after 85 min of irradiation, indicating a low photocatalytic activity of the BP nanosheets. This was ascribed to the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs in the conduction band (CB) and the valence band (VB), as no electron mediator was present to store and shuttle the photogenerated electrons [29]. The Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibits a slightly higher catalytic activity within the first 35 min, which can be ascribed to the strong plasmonic coupling between the neighboring Ag clusters anchored on the TiO2 nanocrystals, which has exhibited a response to the visible-light. Unfortunately, the Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid dramatically lost its catalytic activity and exhibited almost no photocatalytic activity after 35 min, which was ascribed to the occurrence of extensive particle aggregation, as indicated by large particle formation and the subsequent precipitation at the bottom of the reactor. As an obvious contrast, the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite exhibited a much higher performance in the photocatalytic degradation of MB when compared to the pure BP nanosheets and Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid. The high photocatalytic activity of the BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid can be ascribed to the high density dispersion of the Ag/TiO2 nanohybrid on the surface of the BP nanosheets. More importantly, the efficient interfacial electron transfer can make great contribution, which will be confirmed by the following photocurrent tests. Furthermore, the photoctalytic activity of BP-Ag/TiO2 in NIR region was also evaluated in Fig. 9 a, in which the UV–vis absorption spectra of MB solution was measured as a function of irradiation time with BP-Ag/TiO2 as a photocatalyst under >780 nm irradiation. We can clearly find that over 25% of MB was degraded in 85 min, while almost no photocatalytic activity was observed with Ag/TiO2 nanohybrids (Fig. 9b). This was attributed to the NIR light response of BP nanosheets as confirmed by the UV–vis spectrum in Fig. 5a, which sensitized Ag/TiO2 nanohybrids to enhance the photocatalytic activity in NIR light region.

The broadband response of BP nanosheets across the visible and NIR light region has played a key role in the photocatalytic activity enhancement for BP-Ag/TiO2 nanohybrids. The transient photocurrent tests of Ag/TiO2 and BP-Ag/TiO2 under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation were evaluated via several on/off cycles of irradiation as shown in Fig. 12 a. Clearly, the photocurrent of BP-Ag/TiO2 was about 1.6 times higher than that of Ag/TiO2 under >420 nm irradiation, indicating a higher electron-hole separation efficiency and a lower recombination rate of charge carriers for BP-Ag/TiO2. Moreover, we could still observe the photocurrent response for BP-Ag/TiO2 in NIR light, as no photocurrent was tested for pure Ag/TiO2 under >780 nm irradiation. In this case, we conclude that BP nanosheets can fully harvest the visible and NIR light, resulting in separation of electron-hole pairs, and the photogenerated electrons transferred from BP and were stored by the Ag clusters on TiO2. The electron spin resonance (ESR) technique with DMPO was carried out to examine the reactive species that were generated during the photocatalytic process. As shown in Fig. 12b, negligible ESR signals were observed under dark conditions. However, the four-line characteristic ESR signals for DMPO-
O2 − were found under both visible and NIR light irradiation, respectively. This suggested that O2 − species was generated in the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye. In this case, the corresponding photocatalytic mechanism can be concluded in Fig. 13 , upon solar light irradiation, electrons are excited from the VB of BP nanosheets to the CB, while at the same time, Ag clusters work as a cocatalyst for the rapid transfer of the photogenerated electrons from the CB of BP nanosheet and then reduce O2 to O2 − through an ultrafast one electron process. At the same time, the leaving holes on the VB of BP nanosheets would effectively degrade the pollutant and as a result, the recombination of electrons and holes cannot occur [36,37]. In our ternary BP-Ag/TiO2 hybrid system, the highly dispersed Ag clusters enriches the adsorption of the pollutant and the form of O2 − species on their surface simultaneously, resulting in the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity towards the degradation of the pollutant.

In this work, a novel ternary BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite possessing high photocatalytic efficiency under visible and NIR light irradiation was prepared. The nanocomposite, with Ag clusters anchored on TiO2 nanocrystals and then supported on BP nanosheets, has demonstrated successful photodegradation of MB dye under the irradiation of a xenon lamp. During the photocatalytic reaction, about 100% and even 25% of MB was degraded in 85 min under >420 nm and >780 nm irradiation, respectively. The BP nanosheets exhibit a broadband response in visible and even NIR light region, resulting in a higher electron-hole separation efficiency and a lower recombination rate of charge carriers. We believe that the BP-Ag/TiO2 nanocomposite will be an effective photofunctional material in full-spectrum solar energy conversion and opens up a new avenue for the development of solar light driven photocatalysts for the remediation of environmental pollution.
There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61705143/61235012/61378091/61525503/61620106016); the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2018M630979); the National Basic Research Program of China (2015CB352005); Guangdong Natural Science Foundation Innovation Team (2014A030312008); Hong Kong, Macao and Taiwan cooperation innovation platform & major projects of international cooperation in Colleges and Universities in Guangdong Province (2015KGJHZ002); and the Science and Technology Innovation Commission of Shenzhen (KQJSCX20170327151457055/JCYJ20150930104948169/JCYJ20160328144746940/GJHZ20160226202139185).
